q: Alpha decay ($\alpha$ decay) is a process in which an atomic nucleus emits an ___ (helium nucleus).
In alpha decay, the mass number reduces by four and the atomic number reduces by ___.
___ and mass number are conserved in the decay process.
$\beta^-$ decay (beta-minus decay) is a process in which a ___ in an atomic nucleus changes to a proton.
In $\beta^-$ decay, the parent nucleus transforms into the daughter nucleus, with mass number remains unchanged but atomic number increases by ___.
$\beta^+$ decay (beta-plus decay) is a process in which a ___ in an atomic nucleus changes to a neutron.
Positron, the ___ of electron, has the same mass and same magnitude of electric charge as an electron.
The existence of positron was theoretically predicted by Dirac in 1931 and experimentally detected by ___ in 1932.
Neutrino, a fundamental particle as an electron, has no charge and negligibly small ___.
For a nucleus to be stable, there must be an appropriate ratio of ___ and neutrons.
If a nucleus has excess neutrons, ___ takes place.
A nucleus with excess protons undergoes ___.
In gamma decay ($\gamma$ decay), the daughter nucleus resulting from alpha or beta decay is left in an ___ state.
In gamma decay, the atomic number and mass number of the parent and daughter nuclei remain ___.
The ___ is the quantum of the electromagnetic field.
Photons are massless and move at the ___ in vacuum.
The energy of a photon is ___ proportional to the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation associated with it.
Alpha rays consist of ___ nuclei, positively charged particles.
The charge of an $\alpha$ particle is ___.
Alpha rays are the ___ ionizing of the three rays.
Alpha rays are the ___ penetrating of the three rays.
Alpha rays can be stopped by a thick sheet of ___.
Beta rays consist of either electrons or ___ moving with varying speeds.
Beta rays can be stopped by a few millimetres of ___.
___ are electromagnetic waves like light and X-rays, but have much shorter wavelengths.
Intensity of gamma rays can be greatly reduced by several centimetres of ___ but they are never completely stopped.
The rate of decay of a radioactive sample is called ___.
SI unit for activity is ___ ($Bq$).
A unit of activity still being used today is ___ ($Ci$).
The rate of decay (activity) is a characteristic of the radioactive element and it is unaffected by the ___.
The ___ is defined as the time for half the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay.
Radioactive decay is a ___ process: it is impossible to predict when a particular atom will decay.
A ___ is a small amount of radioactive isotope introduced into a system in order to follow the behaviour of some components of that system.
In medicine, radioactive tracers are effectively used in ___ procedures.
Treatment with radiation is known as ___.
Radioisotopes such as ___ and cesium-137 are widely used to treat cancer in radiotherapy.
In industrial gamma radiography, radioactive materials are used to inspect ___ or welded joints.
The ability to use radioisotopes to accurately measure thickness is widely used as ___ system in the production of sheet materials.
___ is a technique for estimating the age of an object by measuring the amounts of various radioisotopes present in it.
Carbon dating was developed by ___.
There is one carbon-14 atom in every trillion ($10^{12}$) of ___ atoms.
___ is the stable final decay product of a series of decays that starts with uranium.
Gamma and neutron irradiation of seeds can be used to induce ___ to develop desired mutants.
Nuclear energy now provides about ___ of the world's electricity.
A ___ is a process in which a particle or a gamma radiation penetrate into a nucleus and changes its configuration.
If the total mass before a nuclear reaction takes place is greater than the total mass after reaction, the reaction is ___.
In an ___ reaction, external energy must be provided for the reaction to take place.
___ is a process where a neutron strikes and penetrates a nucleus of uranium-235, and the nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei.
For a ___ to be maintained, the uranium-235 has to be above a certain critical mass.
The energy released per atom in a nuclear reaction is about a ___ times greater than the energy released per atom from a chemical reaction.
___ is a device in which a fission chain reaction can be initiated, maintained and controlled.
The essential elements of a nuclear reactor are fissionable nuclear fuel, moderator, coolant, control rods, reactor vessel and ___.
___ or plutonium-239 can be used as fuel.
___ is a material used to slow down fast neutrons released from a fission process.
___ and heavy water ($\text{D}_2\text{O}$) are used as moderators.
___ is to extract the heat produced by fission fragments when they slow down and stop in the reactor material.
___ and carbon dioxide $\text{CO}_2$ gas are used as coolant.
___ are used to control the rate of reaction by raising or lowering them from the core.
Boron and ___ (neutron absorbing materials) are used as control rods.
___ (or containment structure) is a protective barrier, usually a dense material.
Materials which are radioactive and for which there is no further use are called ___.
Strontium-90 becomes concentrated in ___ and iodine in the thyroid gland.
___ is highly toxic; if breathed in, a very small amount can kill a person.
In an atomic bomb, an ___ chain reaction takes place.
The detonation of an atomic bomb releases enormous amounts of thermal energy, achieving temperatures of several million degrees ___.
The ___ emissions from nuclear fission power are much smaller than those associated with coal, oil and gas.
The 1986 ___ disaster ended the rapid growth of global nuclear power capacity.
In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed that light must also be thought of as particle or ___.
Louis de Broglie suggested that the momentum $p$ of a particle and its associated wavelength $\lambda$ are related by the equation ___.
The wavelength $\lambda$ associated with a moving particle (matter wave) is also called ___ wavelength.
All moving particles exhibit properties of both ___ and waves nature.
The existence of matter waves were first experimentally confirmed by ___ cathode ray diffraction experiment.
In ___, the wave nature of electrons is used instead of light.
An ___ number of de Broglie wavelengths associated with a revolving electron must fit in the circumference of an orbit.
De Broglie hypothesis proves Bohr’s second postulate, which states that the ___ momentum of the orbiting electron is quantized.
___ is the energy gained by an electron when accelerated through a potential of one volt.
Wave nature cannot be observed for ___ particles like a basketball.
___ was the first to point out that all motion is relative.
The choice of the ___ is arbitrary.
During the 19th century, it was believed that the space that light waves traveled through was filled with an invisible hypothetical material called ___.
Michelson and Morley designed an ___ to detect the existence of ether.
The result of the Michelson-Morley experiment is strong evidence against the prevalent ___ theory.
Einstein’s special theory of relativity is based on ___ simple postulates.
The laws of physics are the same in all ___ frames of reference.
The speed of light in ___ is the same in all inertial frames of reference.
An ___ frame is a frame of reference that has no acceleration.
___ states that two events that are simultaneous in one frame of reference may not be simultaneous in another frame moving relative to the first frame.
___ is the phenomenon of time passing more slowly for an observer who is moving relative to another observer.
The time interval $\Delta t_0$ measured in the astronaut’s frame is known as the ___.
Relativistic effects usually become significant when speeds become comparable to the ___.
___ is the length of an object measured by an observer who is at rest relative to the object.
When an object is moving with speed $v$ parallel to its length, its length is measured to be ___.
___ $m_0$ is measured when a particle is at rest relative to the observer.
Newton’s second law of motion is valid in relativistic mechanics if we introduce the relativistic ___ $\vec{p}$.
A rapidly moving particle increases in ___.
The law of ___ of energy is valid relativistically.
The expression for the total energy of an object of mass $m_0$ at rest is ___.
Special Relativity was published in ___.
General Relativity is a theory of ___.
Special Relativity applies to observers moving at ___ speed.
General Relativity applies to observers that are ___.
